item.title: Ruby 2.7 - Numbered Parameters
item.description:
Mar 17

Ruby 2.7 is coming out this December, as with all modern releases, but that doesnãt stop us from looking for and writing about all the fun things we find in the mean time! No no no.

For this article, we have something thatãs very reminiscent of Bash, Perl, and Scala: Numbered parameters.

The Short Version

If you have a simple block with positional arguments, especially single positional, you can do the following:

[1, 2, 3].map { @1 + 3 }
=> [4, 5, 6]

ãÎwhere @1 is the first parameter to the block function.

The Discussion

If youãd like to see the discussion behind this feature, it can be found here:

Valid Only In Blocks

Numbered parameters are only valid when referenced inside of a block:

assert_syntax_error('@1', /outside block/)
assert_valid_syntax('proc {@1}')

That means this is valid:

proc { @1 }
=> #<Proc:0x0000000111d42990@(pry):6>

ãÎand this will cause a syntax error:

@1
SyntaxError: (eval):2: numbered parameter outside block

Now the error is slightly modified from older Ruby versions in that it recognizes the second-use of the instance variable-like syntax and lets us know we used it outside of a block.

Naming Rules

A numbered param has to follow a few rules, namely there are only numbers in it and 0 along with leading 0s are errors:

assert_syntax_error('proc {@01}', /leading zero/)    assert_syntax_error('proc {@1_}', /unexpected/)

That also means itãs going to do bad things if you try and use underscores for longer numbers:

(1..1_000_000).each_slice(1_000).map { @1 + @1_000 + @2 + @3 }
SyntaxError: unexpected local variable or method, expecting '}'
..._slice(1_000).map { @1 + @1_000 + @2 + @3 }
...                           ^~~~
(eval):2: numbered parameter outside block
...e(1_000).map { @1 + @1_000 + @2 + @3 }
...

Multiple Numbered Parameters

Say we have collections or even hashes, we can use @2 and further if we need them to get at the specific values:

assert_equal(3, eval('[1,2].then {@1+@2}'))
assert_equal("12", eval('[1,2].then {"#@1#@2"}'))

For hashes this means you can access the key and the value:

{name: 'foo', age: 42}.map { [@1, @2] }
=> [[:name, "foo"], [:age, 42]]

If you had groups of three you could even start using more:

(1..9).each_slice(3).map { @1 + @2 + @3 }
=> [6, 15, 24]

ãÎthough it may be ill-advised to start getting into too many of these numbered params, as eventually you run out.

Too Large of a Number

There is a limit, but itãs rather high:

assert_syntax_error('proc {@9999999999999999}', /too large/)

Defined here in this constant:

#define NUMPARAM_MAX 100 /* INT_MAX */

Though there should be warning signs that youãre doing something odd before you get anywhere close to this number.

Currently Pry will just give up if you try, and expect more input:

[13] pry(main)> (1..1_000_000).each_slice(1_000).map { @101 }
[13] pry(main)*

Ordinary Parameters

Ruby doesnãt like mix-and-match with our current way of doing block parameters:

assert_syntax_error('proc {|| @1}', /ordinary parameter is defined/)    assert_syntax_error('proc {|x| @1}', /ordinary parameter is defined/)

If you decide to use this, know that itãs one or the other, not both.

Hashes and Objects

It would be good to remember that @1 and friends are just Ruby objects, meaning we can call anything on them that we would a parameter:

[{name: 'foo'}, {name: 'bar'}].map { @1[:name] }
=> ["foo", "bar"]
[{name: 'foo'}, {name: 'bar'}]
  .map { OpenStruct.new(@1) }
  .map { @1.name }
=> ["foo", "bar"]

Though in the last example, it would be good to remember the current shorthand syntax of map(&:name) instead.

Wrapping Up

This is definitely a very interesting feature, though I certainly feel Matz when he says the following:

I still feel weird when I see @ and @1 etc. Maybe I will get used to it after a while.
I need time.
- Matz.

I wonder what new things it will lead to, but Iãm excited nonetheless to see what else people can use it for.

2.7 is already off to an interesting start, letãs see where it goes from here.

item.description: Comments

item.title: How do you compute the midpoint of an interval with floating point?
item.description: Abstract : The algorithm that computes the midpoint of an interval with floating-point bounds requires some careful devising to correctly handle all possible inputs. We review several implementations from prominent C/C++ interval arithmetic packages and analyze their potential failure to deliver correct results. We then highlight two implementations that avoid common pitfalls. The results presented are also relevant to non-interval arithmetic computation such as the implementation of bisection methods. Enough background on IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic is provided for this paper to serve as a practical introduction to the analysis of floating-point computation.

item.title: A Meta Lesson
item.description:
March 21, 2019

Two giants of AI, Rich Sutton and Rodney Brookes, have recently written essays ostensibly presenting two opposing viewpoints for the future of AI research. I highly recommend reading A Bitter Lesson and the follow up A Better Lesson. Both authors have prided themselves on elegant brevity which is good. But both viewpoints are so rich they really need elaboration. So this attempt at synthesis will be much more verbose but hopefully edifying. Rich Sutton the sage has delivered his teaching, itãs traditional for us junior monks/researchers to provide the accompanying exposition and analysis.

First a bit of background: Rich Sutton, as far as I can tell from studying his work and hearing him speak believes in a kind of reward-minimalism. The idea that everything can and should be learned from a reward signal alone. Give me a world, tell me what is better and what is worse and I will give you an agent. He seems to have a critical view of anything that tries to include any innate skill or ability. For example, why build in SLAM (Simultaneous Location and Mapping)? If the agent needs to locate itself to get a high reward, it will learn to locate. If itãs unnecessary ã it wonãt. If thereãs a better way to get where it needs to go, it will eventually learn that instead of making a map. Why should we corrupt our agents with our own limited ideas about how to solve a problem?

Many reward-minimalists believe that some kind of meta-learning will take place if a sufficiently intelligent agent interacts with a complex environment. I think Rich has a rich and nuanced view on this, which I donãt comprehend. I tried to ask him and David Silver at a NeurIPS panel last year and the question came out all garbled because I was nervous and jet-lagged. Either way, itãs intuitive that an agent shouldnãt just learn how to increase its reward. In a sufficiently rich environment it should improve its methods of learning, e.g. develop curiosity, reasoning etc. This is also related to recusive self-improvement, the idea that an agent could learn to modify itself and that the subsequent better version could do that again, improving each time.

Rodney Brookes on the other hand has long had a chip on his shoulder about the amount of compute needed to do AI. He pioneered building robots with ingenious techniques that use many individually simple rules that could interact and override each other, called a subsumption architecture. The emergent behaviour of these interacting simple rules could robustly solve surprisingly complex problems with very low computational power and primitive sensors. He expressed his contrarian approach in the title of his book ãFast, Cheap and Out of Controlã. He created the iRobot company and the Roomba vacuum cleaner along these principles ã and it worked.

It is therefore not surprising that Rich and Rodney have butted heads on the role of increased computation power for the development of AI. Although I was surprised at Rod for his polemic tone and some mischaracterisations of Richãs arguments. I think that Rich and Rod both take clear positions with strong examples, my main contention would be that Rod discounts using learning and search to improve learning itself and so misses the deeper implications of Richãs position.

Iãm going to try and summarise both their positions in a few sentences, but you should definitely read both essays especially because they are so short.

Rich (approx.): learning and search always outperform hand-crafted solutions given enough compute.

Rodney (approx.): No, human ingenuity is actually responsible for progress in AI. We canãt just solve problems by throwing more compute at them.

I think both positions are interesting, important and well supported by evidence. But if you read both essays, youãll see that these positions are also not mutually exclusive, in fact they are synthetic. But you need to take you view one level ãupã so to speak. Iãll illustrate this with a quote from Rodney:

One of the most celebrated successes of Deep Learning is image labelling, using CNNs, Convolutional Neural Networks, but the very essence of CNNs is that the front end of the network is deigned by humans to manage translational invariance, the idea that objects can appear anywhere in the frame. To have a Deep Learning network also have to learn that seems pedantic to the extreme, and will drive up the computational costs of the learning by many orders of magnitude.

All true. But there is a critical omission: translational invariance isnãt necessarily the only good way to solve vision problems. We donãt want an AI algorithm that will exactly relearn to use convolutions. We want a computer vision system that can learn something even better than convolutions. Maybe some other more efficient sparse connectivity pattern or even an entirely different way to do scene parsing. This is critical, moving past simple feature engineering opened up a whole new world for computer vision. The choice of convolutions is a hand-crafted inductive bias ã a choice that will eventually need to yield to learning as well. But you see what happened, in the past we hand-crafted features, now we hand-craft architectures (A point Rob himself makes) this is exactly what Rich Sutton is critical of; we should be always seeking to do more with learning and search. Indeed, we are using learning and search to improve learning itself, meta-learning and architecture search are already an important and growing area of research. Architecture search is relatively unsophisticated at the moment, but it will hopefully give us something better than just throwing countless PhD students at the problem (an algorithm I callãÎ graduate descent)

Rob goes on to criticise the need for massive data sets in Deep Learning, this is indeed a fair criticism but totally mischaracterises Rich; he doesnãt even use the word ãdataã once in his essay. A robot that could learn to recognise objects from interaction with the world alone is certainly one of their shared goals. Even then, the best one-shot and few-shot learning systems that exist today either make use of meta-learning or learned vector representations. Just to reiterate, the most sample efficient systems that exist today are learned.

Rob writes about the need for computational efficiency, he cites the brain using only 20W. This I think is a strange point, accurate LSTM speech recognition models use very little compute and can run effectively on mobile phones. Training these models may take a very large amount of compute, optimising their hyperparameters, even more. But the deployed model is frugal. Isnãt the total cost of this system when deployed on millions of phones still very good? Googleãs recent AlphaStar model runs on a single GPU when itãs actually playing. Sure, training AlphaStar took a huge amount of power ã improve that! Rich Sutton isnãt arguing for wasteful learning and search, heãs calling on us to improve it. He is saying weãll never be able to go back to hand-written StarCraft bots. Building a StarCraft bot that can rival AlphaStar with only a few hours of play would be a huge achievement for AI, but that would mean better learning and search, exactly what Rich advocates.

If you train an LSTM with reinforcement learning to solve maze after maze it will learn complex navigation behaviour, backtracking to take past turns etc. Incredibly, it learns to learn the layout of each individual maze problem. Does it do SLAM? Does it ãknowã where it is? does it use Bayesian inference? It does do whatever it does, with a surprisingly small amount of compute after training. Learning-to-learn can yield systems that are more efficient than anything we can program. Meta-learning synthesises the need for efficient learning and moving away from ad-hoc hand-crafting.

Letãs elaborate on something from Rich now:

A similar pattern of research progress was seen in computer Go, only delayed by a further 20 years. Enormous initial efforts went into avoiding search by taking advantage of human knowledge, or of the special features of the game, but all those efforts proved irrelevant, or worse, once search was applied effectively at scale. Also important was the use of learning by self play to learn a value function (as it was in many other games and even in chess, although learning did not play a big role in the 1997 program that first beat a world champion). Learning by self play, and learning in general, is like search in that it enables massive computation to be brought to bear. Search and learning are the two most important classes of techniques for utilizing massive amounts of computation in AI research. In computer Go, as in computer chess, researchersã initial effort was directed towards utilizing human understanding (so that less search was needed) and only much later was much greater success had by embracing search and learning.

Rich is referencing the success of AlphaGo and AlphaZero here. I think this example is illustrative. AlphaZero has very little Go, Chess or Shogi specific built into it, aside from being quite amazing it puts some of Rodãs criticisms into focus. It is true that AlphaGo uses a lot of compute to train, but how many thousands of hours were poured into the Stockfish position evaluation function? How many more would be needed to beat AZ? Yes, much research went into AlphaGo itself, but adding Chess and Shogi after Go took a relative small team less than a year, how long would it take to port Stockfish to Shogi? Furthermore, how long would Stockfish need to grind on a move to match the skill of AlphaZero? Once trained, AlphaZeroãs play is more efficient than Stockfish for the same skill level.

I think that Sutton could have been more clear that he does not think more computation is a panacea in-and-of-itself, but Learning and search are naturally and passively improved by increasing computation. He could have emphasised that improving search and learning by increasing the efficiency is an important goal too. Rob was too quick to attack only the compute related portions of Suttonãs essay, what he missed was that learning and search will ultimately improve computational efficiency.

The meta lesson is that the most important thing to improve with search and learning ã is learning itself. Building an agent with real-world recursive self-improvement will require all the compute and ingenuity we can muster.

item.title: How I'm able to take notes in mathematics lectures using LaTeX and Vim
item.description:
A while back I anôÙswered a quesôÙtion on Quora: Can peoôÙple acôÙtuôÙalôÙly keep up with note-ãtaking in MathôÙeôÙmatôÙics lecôÙtures with LaTeX. There, I exôÙplained my workôÙflow of takôÙing lecôÙture notes in LaTeX using Vim and how I draw figôÙures in Inkscape. HowôÙevôÙer, a lot has changed since then and Iãd like to write a few blog posts exôÙplainôÙing my workôÙflow.

I startôÙed using LaTeX to write lecôÙture notes in the secôÙond seôÙmesôÙter of my bachôÙeôÙlor in mathôÙeôÙmatôÙics, and Iãve been using it ever since, which makes for a total of more than 1700 pages of notes. To give you an idea of what those notes look like, here are some exôÙamôÙples:

These lecôÙture notes ã inôÙcludôÙing figôÙures ã are made while atôÙtendôÙing the lecôÙture and have not been editôÙed afôÙterôÙwards. To make note takôÙing using LaTeX viôÙable, I had four goals in mind:

  • WritôÙing text and mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal forôÙmuôÙlas in LaTeX should be as fast as the lecôÙturôÙer writôÙing on a blackôÙboard: no delay is acôÙceptôÙable.
  • DrawôÙing figôÙures should be alôÙmost as fast as the lecôÙturôÙer.
  • ManôÙagôÙing notes, i.e. adding a note, comôÙpilôÙing all my notes, comôÙpilôÙing the last two lecôÙtures, searchôÙing in notes, etc. should be easy and quick.
  • AnôÙnoôÙtatôÙing pdf docôÙuôÙments using LaTeX should be posôÙsiôÙble for when I want to write notes alongôÙside a pdf docôÙuôÙment.

This blog post will focus on the first item: writôÙing LaTeX.

Vim and LaTeX

For writôÙing text and mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal forôÙmuôÙlas in LaTeX, I use Vim. Vim is a powôÙerôÙful genôÙerôÙal purôÙpose text edôÙiôÙtor thatãs very exôÙtenôÙsiôÙble. I use it for writôÙing code, LaTeX, markôÙdown, ãÎ baôÙsiôÙcalôÙly everyôÙthing thatãs text-ãbased. It has a fairôÙly steep learnôÙing curve, but once youãve got the baôÙsics down, itãs hard to get back to an edôÙiôÙtor withôÙout Vim keyôÙbindôÙings. Hereãs what my screen looks like when Iãm editôÙing LaTeX:

On the left you see Vim and on the right my pdf viewôÙer, ZaôÙthuôÙra, which also has Vim-ãlike keyôÙbindôÙings. Iãm using UbunôÙtu with bspwm as my winôÙdow manôÙagôÙer. The LaTeX pluôÙgôÙin Iãm using in Vim is vimôÙtex. It proôÙvides synôÙtax highôÙlightôÙing, table of conôÙtents view, syncôÙtex, etc. Using vim-ãplug, I conôÙfigôÙured it as folôÙlows:

Plug 'lervag/vimtex'
let g:tex_flavor='latex'
let g:vimtex_view_method='zathura'
let g:vimtex_quickfix_mode=0
set conceallevel=1
let g:tex_conceal='abdmg'

The last two lines conôÙfigôÙure the conôÙcealôÙment. This is a feaôÙture where LaTeX code is reôÙplaced or made inôÙvisôÙiôÙble when your curôÙsor is not on that line. By makôÙing \[, \], $ inôÙvisôÙiôÙble, theyãre less obôÙtruôÙsive which gives you a betôÙter overview of the docôÙuôÙment. This feaôÙture also reôÙplaces \bigcap by by ãˋ, \in by ã etc. The folôÙlowôÙing anôÙiôÙmaôÙtion should make that clear.

With this set up, I come to the crux of this blog post: writôÙing LaTeX as fast as the lecôÙturôÙer can write on the blackôÙboard. This is where snipôÙpets come into play.

SnipôÙpets

Whatãs a snipôÙpet?

A snipôÙpet is a short reusable piece of text that can be trigôÙgered by some other text. For exôÙamôÙple, when I type sign and press Tab, the word sign will be exôÙpandôÙed to a sigôÙnaôÙture:

SnipôÙpets can also be dyôÙnamôÙic: when I type today and press Tab, the word today will be reôÙplaced by the curôÙrent date, and box Tab beôÙcomes a box that auôÙtoôÙmatôÙiôÙcalôÙly grows in size.

You can even use one snipôÙpet inôÙside anôÙothôÙer:

Using UlôÙtiSnips to creôÙate snipôÙpets

I use the pluôÙgôÙin UlôÙtiSnips to manôÙage my snipôÙpets. My conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion is

Plug 'sirver/ultisnips'
let g:UltiSnipsExpandTrigger = '<tab>'
let g:UltiSnipsJumpForwardTrigger = '<tab>'
let g:UltiSnipsJumpBackwardTrigger = '<s-tab>'

The code for the sign snipôÙpet is the folôÙlowôÙing:

snippet sign "Signature"
Yours sincerely,

Gilles Castel
endsnippet

For dyôÙnamôÙic snipôÙpets, you can put code beôÙtween backôÙticks `` which will be run when the snipôÙpet is exôÙpandôÙed. Here, Iãve used bash to forôÙmat the curôÙrent date: date + %F.

snippet today "Date"
`date +%F`
endsnippet

You can also use Python inôÙside a `!p ... ` block. Have a look at the code for the box snipôÙpet:

snippet box "Box"
`!p snip.rv = 'ã' + 'ã' * (len(t[1]) + 2) + 'ã'`
ã $1 ã
`!p snip.rv = 'ã' + 'ã' * (len(t[1]) + 2) + 'ã'`
$0
endsnippet

These Python code blocks will be reôÙplaced by the value of the variôÙable snip.rv. InôÙside these blocks, you have acôÙcess to the curôÙrent state of the snipôÙpet, e.g. t[1] conôÙtains the first tab stop, fn the curôÙrent fileôÙname, ãÎ

LaTeX snipôÙpets

Using snipôÙpets, writôÙing LaTeX is a lot faster than writôÙing it by hand. EsôÙpeôÙcialôÙly some of the more comôÙplex snipôÙpets can save you a lot of time and frusôÙtraôÙtion. Letãs begin with some simôÙple snipôÙpets.

EnôÙviôÙronôÙments

To inôÙsert an enôÙviôÙronôÙment, all I have to do is type beg at the beôÙginôÙning of a line. Then I type the name of the enôÙviôÙronôÙment, which is mirôÙrored in the \end{} comôÙmand. PressôÙing Tab places the curôÙsor inôÙside the newly creôÙatôÙed enôÙviôÙronôÙment.

The code for this snipôÙpet is the folôÙlowôÙing.

snippet beg "begin{} / end{}" bA
\begin{$1}
	$0
\end{$1}
endsnippet

The b means that this snipôÙpet will only be exôÙpandôÙed at the beôÙginôÙning of a line and A stands for auto exôÙpand, which means I do not have to press Tab to exôÙpand the snipôÙpet. Tab stops ã i.e. places you can jump to by pressôÙing Tab and Shift+Tab ã are repôÙreôÙsentôÙed by $1, $2, ãÎ and the last one with $0.

InôÙline and disôÙplay math

Two of my most freôÙquentôÙly used snipôÙpets are mk and dm. Theyãre the snipôÙpets reôÙsponôÙsiôÙble for startôÙing math mode. The first one is a snipôÙpet for inôÙline math, the secôÙond one for disôÙplayed math.

The snipôÙpet for inôÙline math is ãsmartã: it knows when to inôÙsert a space after the dolôÙlar sign. When I start typôÙing a word diôÙrectôÙly beôÙhind the closôÙing $, it adds a space. HowôÙevôÙer, when I type a non-ãword charôÙacôÙter, it does not add a space, which would be preôÙferred for exôÙamôÙple in the case of ã

p

p

-ãvalueã.

The code for this snipôÙpet is the folôÙlowôÙing.

snippet mk "Math" wA
$${1}$`!p
if t[2] and t[2][0] not in [',', '.', '?', '-', ' ']:
    snip.rv = ' '
else:
    snip.rv = ''
`$2
endsnippet

The w at the end of the first line means that this snipôÙpet will exôÙpand at word boundôÙaries, so e.g. hellomk wonãt exôÙpand, but hello mk will.

The snipôÙpet for disôÙplayed math is more simôÙple, but it also is quite handy; it makes me never forôÙget endôÙing equaôÙtions with a peôÙriôÙod.

snippet dm "Math" wA
\[
$1
.\] $0
endsnippet

Sub- and suôÙperôÙscripts

AnôÙothôÙer useôÙful snipôÙpet is one for subôÙscripts. It changes changes a1 to a_1 and a_12 to a_{12}.

The code for this snipôÙpet uses a regôÙuôÙlar exôÙpresôÙsion for its trigôÙger. It exôÙpands the snipôÙpet when you type a charôÙacôÙter folôÙlowed by a digit, which enôÙcodôÙed by [A-Za-z]\d, or a charôÙacôÙter folôÙlowed by _ and two digôÙits: [A-Za-z]_\d\d.

snippet '([A-Za-z])(\d)' "auto subscript" wrA
`!p snip.rv = match.group(1)`_`!p snip.rv = match.group(2)`
endsnippet

snippet '([A-Za-z])_(\d\d)' "auto subscript2" wrA
`!p snip.rv = match.group(1)`_{`!p snip.rv = match.group(2)`}
endsnippet

When you wrap parts of a regôÙuôÙlar exôÙpresôÙsion in a group using parenôÙtheôÙsis, e.g. (\d\d), you can use them in the exôÙpanôÙsion of the snipôÙpet via match.group(i) in Python.

As for suôÙperôÙscripts, I use td, which beôÙcomes ^{}. HowôÙevôÙer, for squared, cubed, comôÙpleôÙment and a handôÙful of other comôÙmon ones, I use dedôÙiôÙcatôÙed snipôÙpets such as sr, cb and comp.

snippet sr "^2" iA
^2
endsnippet

snippet cb "^3" iA
^3
endsnippet

snippet compl "complement" iA
^{c}
endsnippet

snippet td "superscript" iA
^{$1}$0
endsnippet

FracôÙtions

One of my most conôÙveôÙnient snipôÙpets is one for fracôÙtions. This makes the folôÙlowôÙing exôÙpanôÙsions:
// ã \frac{}{}
3/ ã \frac{3}{}
4\pi^2/ ã \frac{4\pi^2}{}
(1 + 2 + 3)/ ã \frac{1 + 2 + 3}{}
(1+(2+3)/) ã (1 + \frac{2+3}{})
(1 + (2+3))/ ã \frac{1 + (2+3)}{}

The code for the first one is easy:

snippet // "Fraction" iA
\\frac{$1}{$2}$0
endsnippet

The secôÙond and third exôÙamôÙples are made posôÙsiôÙble using regôÙuôÙlar exôÙpresôÙsions to match for exôÙpresôÙsions like 3/, 4ac/, 6\pi^2/, a_2/, etc.

snippet '((\d+)|(\d*)(\\)?([A-Za-z]+)((\^|_)(\{\d+\}|\d))*)/' "Fraction" wrA
\\frac{`!p snip.rv = match.group(1)`}{$1}$0
endsnippet

As you can see, regôÙuôÙlar exôÙpresôÙsions can beôÙcome quite overôÙwhelmôÙing, but hereãs a diôÙaôÙgram that should exôÙplain it:

In the fourth and fifth cases, it tries to find the matchôÙing parenôÙtheôÙsis. As this isnãt posôÙsiôÙble using the regôÙuôÙlar exôÙpresôÙsion enôÙgine of UlôÙtiSnips, I reôÙsortôÙed to using Python:

priority 1000
snippet '^.*\)/' "() Fraction" wrA
`!p
stripped = match.string[:-1]
depth = 0
i = len(stripped) - 1
while True:
	if stripped[i] == ')': depth += 1
	if stripped[i] == '(': depth -= 1
	if depth == 0: break;
	i -= 1
snip.rv = stripped[0:i] + "\\frac{" + stripped[i+1:-1] + "}"
`{$1}$0
endsnippet

The last snipôÙpet conôÙcernôÙing fracôÙtions Iãd like to share is one that uses your seôÙlecôÙtion to make a fracôÙtion. You can use it by first seôÙlectôÙing some text, then pressôÙing Tab, typôÙing / and pressôÙing Tab again.

The code makes use of the ${VISUAL} variôÙable that repôÙreôÙsents your seôÙlecôÙtion.

snippet / "Fraction" iA
\\frac{${VISUAL}}{$1}$0
endsnippet

Sympy and MathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙca

AnôÙothôÙer cool ã but less used ã snipôÙpet is one that uses sympy to evalôÙuôÙate mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal exôÙpresôÙsions. For exôÙamôÙple: sympy Tab exôÙpands to sympy | sympy, and sympy 1 + 1 sympy Tab exôÙpands to 2.

snippet sympy "sympy block " w
sympy $1 sympy$0
endsnippet

priority 10000
snippet 'sympy(.*)sympy' "evaluate sympy" wr
`!p
from sympy import *
x, y, z, t = symbols('x y z t')
k, m, n = symbols('k m n', integer=True)
f, g, h = symbols('f g h', cls=Function)
init_printing()
snip.rv = eval('latex(' + match.group(1).replace('\\', '') \
    .replace('^', '**') \
    .replace('{', '(') \
    .replace('}', ')') + ')')
`
endsnippet

For the MathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙca users out there, you can do someôÙthing simôÙiôÙlar:

priority 1000
snippet math "mathematica block" w
math $1 math$0
endsnippet

priority 10000
snippet 'math(.*)math' "evaluate mathematica" wr
`!p
import subprocess
code = 'ToString[' + match.group(1) + ', TeXForm]'
snip.rv = subprocess.check_output(['wolframscript', '-code', code])
`
endsnippet

PostôÙfix snipôÙpets

Some other snipôÙpets I find worth sharôÙing are postôÙfix snipôÙpets. ExôÙamôÙples of such snipôÙpets are phat ã \hat{p} and zbar ã \overline{z}. A simôÙiôÙlar snipôÙpet is a postôÙfix vecôÙtor, for exôÙamôÙple v,. ã \vec{v} and v., ã \vec{v}. The order of , and . doesnãt matôÙter, so I can press them both at the same time. These snipôÙpets are a real time-ãsaver, beôÙcause you can type in the same order the lecôÙturôÙer writes on the blackôÙboard.

Note that I can still use bar and hat preôÙfix too, as Iãve added them with a lower priôÙorôÙiôÙty. The code for those snipôÙpets is:

priority 10
snippet "bar" "bar" riA
\overline{$1}$0
endsnippet

priority 100
snippet "([a-zA-Z])bar" "bar" riA
\overline{`!p snip.rv=match.group(1)`}
endsnippet
priority 10
snippet "hat" "hat" riA
\hat{$1}$0
endsnippet

priority 100
snippet "([a-zA-Z])hat" "hat" riA
\hat{`!p snip.rv=match.group(1)`}
endsnippet
snippet "(\\?\w+)(,\.|\.,)" "Vector postfix" riA
\vec{`!p snip.rv=match.group(1)`}
endsnippet

Other snipôÙpets

I have about 100 other comôÙmonôÙly used snipôÙpets. They are availôÙable here. Most of them are quite simôÙple. For exôÙamôÙple, !> beôÙcomes \mapsto, -> beôÙcomes \to, etc.

fun beôÙcomes f: \R \to \R :, !> ã \mapsto, -> ã \to, cc ã \subset.

lim beôÙcomes \lim_{n \to \infty}, sum ã \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty}, ooo ã \infty

Course speôÙcifôÙic snipôÙpets

BeôÙside my comôÙmonôÙly used snipôÙpets, I also have course speôÙcifôÙic snipôÙpets. These are loaded by adding the folôÙlowôÙing to my .vimrc:

set rtp+=~/current_course

where current_course is a symôÙlink to my curôÙrentôÙly acôÙtiôÙvatôÙed course (more about that in anôÙothôÙer blog post). In that foldôÙer, I have a file ~/current_course/UltiSnips/tex.snippets in which I inôÙclude course speôÙcifôÙic snipôÙpets. For exôÙamôÙple, for quanôÙtum meôÙchanôÙics, I have snipôÙpets for bra/ket noôÙtaôÙtion.
<a| ã \bra{a}
<q| ã \bra{\psi}
|a> ã \ket{a}
|q> ã \ket{\psi}
<a|b> ã \braket{a}{b}

As \psi is used a lot in quanôÙtum meôÙchanôÙics, I reôÙplace all inôÙstances of q in a braket with \psi when exôÙpandôÙed.

snippet "\<(.*?)\|" "bra" riA
\bra{`!p snip.rv = match.group(1).replace('q', f'\psi').replace('f', f'\phi')`}
endsnippet

snippet "\|(.*?)\>" "ket" riA
\ket{`!p snip.rv = match.group(1).replace('q', f'\psi').replace('f', f'\phi')`}
endsnippet

snippet "(.*)\\bra{(.*?)}([^\|]*?)\>" "braket" riA
`!p snip.rv = match.group(1)`\braket{`!p snip.rv = match.group(2)`}{`!p snip.rv = match.group(3).replace('q', f'\psi').replace('f', f'\phi')`}
endsnippet

ConôÙtext

One thing to conôÙsidôÙer when writôÙing these snipôÙpets is, ãwill these snipôÙpets colôÙlide with usual text?ã For exôÙamôÙple, acôÙcordôÙing to my dicôÙtioôÙnary, there are about 72 words in EngôÙlish and 2000 words in Dutch that conôÙtain sr, which means that while Iãm typôÙing the word disregard, the sr would exôÙpand to ^2, givôÙing me di^2egard.

The soôÙluôÙtion to this probôÙlem is adding a conôÙtext to snipôÙpets. Using the synôÙtax highôÙlightôÙing of Vim, it can be deôÙterôÙmined whether or not UlôÙtiSnips should exôÙpand the snipôÙpet deôÙpendôÙing if youãre in math or text. I came up with the folôÙlowôÙing:

global !p
texMathZones = ['texMathZone'+x for x in ['A', 'AS', 'B', 'BS', 'C',
'CS', 'D', 'DS', 'E', 'ES', 'F', 'FS', 'G', 'GS', 'H', 'HS', 'I', 'IS',
'J', 'JS', 'K', 'KS', 'L', 'LS', 'DS', 'V', 'W', 'X', 'Y', 'Z']]

texIgnoreMathZones = ['texMathText']

texMathZoneIds = vim.eval('map('+str(texMathZones)+", 'hlID(v:val)')")
texIgnoreMathZoneIds = vim.eval('map('+str(texIgnoreMathZones)+", 'hlID(v:val)')")

ignore = texIgnoreMathZoneIds[0]

def math():
	synstackids = vim.eval("synstack(line('.'), col('.') - (col('.')>=2 ? 1 : 0))")
	try:
		first = next(
            i for i in reversed(synstackids)
            if i in texIgnoreMathZoneIds or i in texMathZoneIds
        )
		return first != ignore
	except StopIteration:
		return False
endglobal

Now you can add context "math()" to the snipôÙpets youãd only want to exôÙpand in a mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal conôÙtext.

context "math()"
snippet sr "^2" iA
^2
endsnippet

Note that a ãmathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal conôÙtextã is a subôÙtle thing. SomeôÙtimes you add some text inôÙside a math enôÙviôÙronôÙment by using \text{...}. In that case, you do not want snipôÙpets to exôÙpand. HowôÙevôÙer, in the folôÙlowôÙing case: \[ \text{$...$} \], they should exôÙpand. This is why the code for the math conôÙtext is a bit comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed. The folôÙlowôÙing anôÙiôÙmaôÙtion ilôÙlusôÙtrates these subôÙtleties.

CorôÙrectôÙing spelling misôÙtakes on the fly

While inôÙsertôÙing mathôÙeôÙmatôÙics is an imôÙporôÙtant part of my note-ãtaking setup, most of the time Iãm typôÙing EngôÙlish. At about 80 words per minute, my typôÙing skills are not bad, but I still make a lot of typos. This is why I added a keyôÙbindôÙing to Vim that corôÙrects the spelling misôÙtakes, withôÙout inôÙterôÙruptôÙing my flow. When I press Ctrl+L while Iãm typôÙing, the preôÙviôÙous spelling misôÙtake is corôÙrectôÙed. It looks like this:

My conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion for spell check is the folôÙlowôÙing:

setlocal spell
set spelllang=nl,en_gb
inoremap <C-l> <c-g>u<Esc>[s1z=`]a<c-g>u

It baôÙsiôÙcalôÙly jumps to the preôÙviôÙous spelling misôÙtake [s, then picks the first sugôÙgesôÙtion 1z=, and then jumps back `]a. The <c-g>u in the midôÙdle make it posôÙsiôÙble to undo the spelling corôÙrecôÙtion quickôÙly.

In conôÙcluôÙsion

Using snipôÙpets in Vim, writôÙing LaTeX is no longer an anôÙnoyôÙance, but rather a pleaôÙsure. In comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion with spell check on the fly, it alôÙlows for a comôÙfortôÙable mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal note-ãtaking setup. A few pieces are missôÙing though, for exôÙamôÙple drawôÙing figôÙures digôÙiôÙtalôÙly and emôÙbedôÙding them in a LaTeX docôÙuôÙment. This is a topic Iãd like to tackôÙle in a fuôÙture blog post.

Written by Gilles Castel, who lives in Belgium studying mathematics at the university of Leuven.
ôˋ 2019 All rights reserved.

item.title: Facebook Stored Hundreds of Millions of User Passwords in Plain Text for Years
item.description:
21
Mar 19

Facebook Stored Hundreds of Millions of User Passwords in Plain Text for Years

Hundreds of millions of Facebook users had their account passwords stored in plain text and searchable by thousands of Facebook employees ã in some cases going back to 2012, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. Facebook says an ongoing investigation has so far found no indication that employees have abused access to this data.

Facebook is probing a series of security failures in which employees built applications that logged unencrypted password data for Facebook users and stored it in plain text on internal company servers. Thatãs according to a senior Facebook employee who is familiar with the investigation and who spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to speak to the press.

The Facebook source said the investigation so far indicates between 200 million and 600 million Facebook users may have had their account passwords stored in plain text and searchable by more than 20,000 Facebook employees. The source said Facebook is still trying to determine how many passwords were exposed and for how long, but so far the inquiry has uncovered archives with plain text user passwords in them dating back to 2012.

My Facebook insider said access logs showed some 2,000 engineers or developers made approximately nine million internal queries for data elements that contained plain text user passwords.

ãThe longer we go into this analysis the more comfortable the legal people [at Facebook] are going with the lower boundsã of affected users, the source said. ãRight now theyãre working on an effort to reduce that number even more by only counting things we have currently in our data warehouse.ã

In an interview with KrebsOnSecurity, Facebook software engineer Scott Renfro said the company wasnãt ready to talk about specific numbers ã such as the number of Facebook employees who could have accessed the data.

Renfro said the company planned to alert affected Facebook users, but that no password resets would be required.

ãWeãve not found any cases so far in our investigations where someone was looking intentionally for passwords, nor have we found signs of misuse of this data,ã Renfro said. ãIn this situation what weãve found is these passwords were inadvertently logged but that there was no actual risk thatãs come from this. We want to make sure weãre reserving those steps and only force a password change in cases where thereãs definitely been signs of abuse.ã

A written statement from Facebook provided to KrebsOnSecurity says the company expects to notify ãhundreds of millions of Facebook Lite users, tens of millions of other Facebook users, and tens of thousands of Instagram users.ã Facebook Lite is a version of Facebook designed for low speed connections and low-spec phones.

Both Github and Twitter were forced to admit similar stumbles in recent months, but in both of those cases the plain text user passwords were available to a relatively small number of people within those organizations, and for far shorter periods of time.

Renfro said the issue first came to light in January 2019 when security engineers reviewing some new code noticed passwords were being inadvertently logged in plain text.

ãThis prompted the team to set up a small task force to make sure we did a broad-based review of anywhere this might be happening,ã Renfro said. ãWe have a bunch of controls in place to try to mitigate these problems, and weãre in the process of investigating long-term infrastructure changes to prevent this going forward. Weãre now reviewing any logs we have to see if there has been abuse or other access to that data.ã

Facebookãs password woes come amid a tough month for the social network. Last week, The New York Times reported that federal prosecutors are conducting a criminal investigation into data deals Facebook struck with some of the worldãs largest tech companies.

Earlier in March, Facebook came under fire from security and privacy experts for using phone numbers provided for security reasons ã like two-factor authentication ã for other things (like marketing, advertising and making users searchable by their phone numbers across the social networkãs different platforms).

Update, 11:43 a.m.: Facebook has posted a statement about this incident here.
